---
title: "A primer: programs, gradients, and the machines that run them"
---

`nitrix` is a library of *differentiable* numerical primitives: every public
function is a pure map over arrays that you can not only **run**, but also
**compile** to a GPU and **differentiate** — automatically, exactly, and at scale.
The other three units of this book (geometry, registration, statistics) use those
three capabilities on every page without dwelling on them. This unit is where we
earn them. It answers four questions a reader meeting differentiable numerics for
the first time should have: *what is the machine underneath, how does a
high-level Python function become fast code on it, what does it mean to
differentiate a program, and why does any of this help a model learn?*

You need linear algebra (matrices, transposes, rank, the inner product) and basic
multivariate calculus (partial derivatives, the chain rule). You do **not** need
differential geometry, tangent bundles, Lie theory, or a background in numerical
methods or GPU programming — each of those ideas is built here from what you
already own, the first time it is needed. The register is the same as the rest of
the book: intuition first, then the earned equation, then the real `nitrix`
routine.

## The three threads {#sec-threads}

Three ideas recur on nearly every page of this unit, and they are worth meeting
once, up front. Everything else hangs on them.

### Thread 1 — one trace, many interpretations {#sec-thread-trace}

A `nitrix` function is a **pure function**: same inputs, same outputs, no hidden
state. That restraint buys something large. A pure function can be *traced* once —
run symbolically on stand-in values — into a small data structure (JAX calls it a
`jaxpr`) that records exactly which primitive operations happen in which order.
Once you hold the program as data, you can **reinterpret** it. Run it as written.
Hand it to the **XLA compiler**, which fuses and schedules it into fast GPU code.
**Linearise** it, to get the map that propagates a small input perturbation
forward. **Transpose** that linear map, to get the one that propagates a
sensitivity backward — which is exactly the gradient. `jit` (compile) and `grad`
(differentiate) are not two unrelated features; they are two *interpretations of
the same trace*, and this unit treats them as such.

### Thread 2 — linearisation is the universal tool {#sec-thread-linear}

The derivative of a function is the **best linear map** that approximates it near a
point — nothing more exotic than that, even when the function maps thousands of
numbers to thousands of numbers. Every differentiation idea in this unit is linear
algebra on these local linear maps: the **Jacobian** is the matrix of the linear
map; the **Jacobian–vector product** (JVP) applies it to a perturbation; the
**vector–Jacobian product** (VJP) applies its *transpose* to a sensitivity; "**you
only linearise once**" is the observation that reverse-mode autodiff is just *the
transpose of the forward linear map*; and the **implicit function theorem** lets us
differentiate things defined by a condition (a root, an optimum, an ODE solution)
by linearising that condition and solving one linear system. We use **Einstein
index notation** throughout, because it makes the multivariate bookkeeping —
which index sums against which — visible and routine.

### Thread 3 — structure is leverage {#sec-thread-structure}

The same word, **inductive bias**, names two payoffs that look unrelated until you
hold them together. Structure in the *computation* buys **performance**: a fused
kernel that respects the memory hierarchy, a sparse operator that skips the zeros,
a *semiring* that replaces $(+,\times)$ with $(\max,+)$ to turn a matrix product
into a shortest-path solve. Structure in the *data* buys **generalisation**: a
model that is told the answer cannot depend on where an object sits in an image, or
on how the nodes of a graph are numbered, needs far less data to learn. Both are
the deliberate exploitation of known structure, and both are what the rest of the
book is about — the geometry unit's symmetry-respecting operators, the registration
unit's Lie-group transforms, the statistics unit's penalties-as-priors. This unit's
final chapter is the bridge to them.

## What `nitrix` is {#sec-library}

`nitrix` is the **lowest-level numerical layer** of the *diffprog* neuroimaging
stack — a pure-[JAX](https://docs.jax.dev) library of differentiable primitives
that higher-level libraries (`hypercoil`, `entense`, and others) build on. Three
commitments shape everything in it, and each is a concrete instance of the threads
above.

- **Everything is a pure function over arrays.** No module objects, no hidden
  state, no I/O — a public symbol takes arrays (or PyTrees of arrays, optionally a
  random key or a static shape/configuration) and returns arrays. This is what
  makes the whole library traceable, hence `jit`-able, `vmap`-able, and
  differentiable end-to-end (Thread 1).
- **Precision and correctness come first.** The scientific core is `float32`/
  `float64`; reduced precision is admitted only at a narrow neural-network seam,
  under a hard rule that accumulation stays at least `float32`. A fused GPU kernel
  always ships beside a plain-JAX **reference** that is certified equal to it within
  a published tolerance, so the fast path can never silently diverge from the
  honest one.
- **Performance is hardware-aware but portable.** `nitrix` targets Ampere-and-newer
  NVIDIA GPUs through hand-written kernels, with a pure-JAX CPU fallback as the
  correctness floor. Crucially, it is designed to run *without* the vendor GPU
  solver (cuSOLVER), which is fragile across deployment environments — a recurring
  motivation you will see paid off in the [implicit-differentiation](implicit-differentiation.qmd)
  chapter and throughout the statistics unit.

A `nitrix` call chooses its behaviour along four *orthogonal* axes, a vocabulary
worth having early because it recurs: the **backend** (which execution engine —
a fused CUDA kernel or portable JAX), the **driver** (which numerically-distinct
recipe of the same maths), the **method** (which algorithm family), and the
**dtype** (which precision). Keeping these separate is what lets one function serve
a laptop CPU and a datacentre GPU from the same source.

## How a program becomes fast *and* differentiable {#sec-trace}

The unifying picture of the unit is @fig-trace: a single pure function, traced
once into a `jaxpr`, then sent down four different paths. The left two are about
*speed* (the [hardware](hardware.qmd) and [XLA/JAX](xla-jax.qmd) chapters); the
right two are about *gradients* (the [differentiation](differentiation.qmd) and
[differentiable-programming](differentiable-programming.qmd) chapters). The whole
unit is an elaboration of this diagram.

::: {#fig-trace}
<svg viewBox="0 0 800 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="A pure function is traced once into a jaxpr, which is then reinterpreted four ways: evaluate, compile, linearise, and transpose."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="14">
  <defs>
    <marker id="ah" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7"
            markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#55606b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- pure function -->
  <rect x="20" y="120" width="150" height="60" rx="6" fill="#dfe7f0"
        stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="95" y="145" text-anchor="middle">pure function</text>
  <text x="95" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-style="italic">f(x) → y</text>
  <!-- trace arrow -->
  <line x1="170" y1="150" x2="250" y2="150" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4"
        marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
  <text x="210" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-style="italic"
        fill="#444b53">trace</text>
  <!-- jaxpr -->
  <rect x="250" y="120" width="150" height="60" rx="6" fill="#fbf0d6"
        stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="325" y="145" text-anchor="middle">the trace</text>
  <text x="325" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-style="italic">(a jaxpr)</text>
  <!-- four interpretations -->
  <g>
    <rect x="500" y="20" width="280" height="46" rx="6" fill="#dce9dd"
          stroke="#33373d"/>
    <text x="512" y="40">evaluate</text>
    <text x="512" y="58" font-size="12" fill="#444b53">run it as written</text>
    <rect x="500" y="86" width="280" height="46" rx="6" fill="#dce9dd"
          stroke="#33373d"/>
    <text x="512" y="106">compile — jit → XLA → GPU</text>
    <text x="512" y="124" font-size="12" fill="#444b53">fuse &amp; schedule for speed</text>
    <rect x="500" y="168" width="280" height="46" rx="6" fill="#e8ddec"
          stroke="#33373d"/>
    <text x="512" y="188">linearise — jvp (pushforward)</text>
    <text x="512" y="206" font-size="12" fill="#444b53">perturbation → forward</text>
    <rect x="500" y="234" width="280" height="46" rx="6" fill="#e8ddec"
          stroke="#33373d"/>
    <text x="512" y="254">transpose — vjp (pullback)</text>
    <text x="512" y="272" font-size="12" fill="#444b53">sensitivity → backward = grad</text>
  </g>
  <!-- fan-out arrows from jaxpr -->
  <line x1="400" y1="150" x2="500" y2="43"  stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
  <line x1="400" y1="150" x2="500" y2="109" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
  <line x1="400" y1="150" x2="500" y2="191" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
  <line x1="400" y1="150" x2="500" y2="257" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
</svg>

One trace, four interpretations. A pure function is traced once into a
`jaxpr`; evaluating, compiling, linearising, and transposing are all
reinterpretations of that single object. The two upper paths are this unit's
"speed" story; the two lower paths are its "gradient" story — and the fourth is
literally the transpose of the third.
:::

@fig-trace also previews the unit's most useful single sentence, made precise in
[the differentiation chapter](differentiation.qmd#sec-yolo): **you only linearise
once.** Reverse-mode automatic differentiation — the engine behind essentially all
deep learning — does not re-derive anything. It linearises the program once to get
a forward linear map, and then *transposes* that map. Speed and gradients turn out
to be the same skill — reinterpreting a trace — applied with different intent.

## Chapters {#sec-chapters}

The unit climbs from the silicon up to the ideas, then hands off to the rest of the
book. Read in order for the full ascent; each chapter assumes the ones before it.

1. [**Programming the GPU**](hardware.qmd) — what a GPU is and why it is fast
   (throughput over latency, the SIMT execution model, the memory hierarchy), the
   **roofline** model of when a computation is compute- or memory-bound, and the
   generations of NVIDIA architecture (Volta → Ampere → Hopper → Blackwell) and
   which of them `nitrix` targets.
2. [**JAX and XLA: levels of representation**](xla-jax.qmd) — pure functions and the
   trace; the ladder of representations a program descends (`jaxpr` → HLO →
   optimised HLO → GPU assembly); how the XLA compiler wins through **operator
   fusion**; and `jit`/`vmap`/`grad` as transformations of one trace.
3. [**Differentiation as linear algebra**](differentiation.qmd) — the derivative as
   the best linear map; **Einstein notation**; the Jacobian, the **JVP**
   (pushforward) and the **VJP** (pullback = transpose); when forward beats reverse
   and vice versa; and "you only linearise once".
4. [**Three kernels, forward and back**](three-kernels.qmd) — the previous chapter
   made concrete on three workhorses: matrix multiply (**gemm**), **softmax**, and
   the **semiring** matrix product, each with its forward pass, its JVP, and its VJP
   worked out in index notation.
5. [**The differentiable-programming paradigm**](differentiable-programming.qmd) —
   the three-step loop (hypothesis class → objective → optimise by autodiff);
   automatic differentiation contrasted with the numerical and symbolic
   alternatives; forward versus reverse mode at program scale; and how real
   frameworks implement it.
6. [**Implicit differentiation and the IFT**](implicit-differentiation.qmd) —
   differentiating through things defined by a *condition* rather than a formula,
   via the implicit function theorem, with two worked examples: the Hermitian
   eigendecomposition and the simplest ordinary differential equation.
7. [**Inductive bias, invariance, and equivariance**](inductive-bias.qmd) — a
   teaser for the rest of the book: hypothesis classes and generalisation, inductive
   bias, and the geometric-deep-learning notions of **invariance** and
   **equivariance** that organise the geometry, registration, and statistics units.

## Reading paths {#sec-paths}

- **Just here to use the library.** Read this page and skim
  [JAX and XLA](xla-jax.qmd) for the `pure function → jit/vmap/grad` workflow and
  its control-flow constraints. That is enough to call `nitrix` correctly and fast.
- **Here for the gradients.** Read [differentiation](differentiation.qmd) and
  [three kernels](three-kernels.qmd) in full, then
  [implicit differentiation](implicit-differentiation.qmd). This is the
  mathematical core, and it is self-contained.
- **Here for the whole picture.** Read in chapter order. The unit is built so that
  the speed story (chapters 1–2) and the gradient story (chapters 3–6) converge on
  the single idea of reinterpreting a trace, and the final chapter (7) opens the
  door to the rest of the book.

## See also

- The library surface: [`nitrix`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix) and its
  architectural record, `SPEC.md`.
- The framework this unit explains: the
  [JAX documentation](https://docs.jax.dev) (transformations, autodiff, and the
  `jaxpr`/XLA pipeline).
- The units this one feeds: [discrete geometry](../geometry/index.qmd),
  [image registration](../registration/index.qmd), and
  [statistical modelling](../statistics/index.qmd).
